era_context: The early 20th century was defined by intensifying imperial competition, rapid industrialisation, and the emergence of new great powers challenging the European-dominated world order. The scramble for Africa neared completion, the Ottoman and Qing empires were in advanced decline, and Japan's dramatic modernisation since the Meiji Restoration was about to be tested in war against Russia. In Europe, the alliance system that would produce the First World War was crystallising, with the Anglo-French Entente Cordiale of 1904 marking a decisive diplomatic realignment. Colonial violence reached genocidal scale in parts of Africa, while technological innovation — from submarines to radio to early electronics — was transforming both warfare and civilian life.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Sub-Saharan Africa beyond colonial administrative records
Central Asia under Russian imperial rule
Indigenous perspectives in settler-colonial states (Australia, Canada, United States)
Rural South and Southeast Asia outside colonial administrative centres
Internal dynamics of the late Qing hinterland beyond treaty ports

events.title: Japan launches surprise attack on Port Arthur; Russo-Japanese War begins
events.description: On the night of 8–9 February 1904, the Imperial Japanese Navy launched a surprise torpedo attack on the Russian Pacific Fleet anchored at Port Arthur (Lüshun) on the Liaodong Peninsula, inflicting significant damage before a formal declaration of war. The attack marked the beginning of the Russo-Japanese War, driven by competing imperial ambitions in Manchuria and Korea. Japan sought to check Russian expansion into areas it considered vital to its security and economic interests. The war would prove to be the first major conflict in which a non-European power defeated a European great power in modern warfare, with profound consequences for the global balance of power, anti-colonial movements across Asia, and the internal stability of the Russian Empire.
key_figures: Admiral Tōgō Heihachirō (commander of the Combined Fleet)
Admiral Stark (Russian commander at Port Arthur)

sources.name: Official Japanese naval records and dispatches, February 1904
The Times (London), war correspondent dispatches, February–March 1904
Rotem Kowner, The A to Z of the Russo-Japanese War (2009)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented by both belligerents and international press correspondents. Exact timing and damage assessments are well established.

events.title: Herero uprising begins; Germany launches genocidal campaign in South-West Africa
events.description: In January 1904, the Herero people, led by Paramount Chief Samuel Maharero, rose against German colonial rule in South-West Africa, driven by systematic land confiscation, forced labour, and sexual violence by settlers. The initial uprising was militarily effective, killing over 100 German settlers and soldiers while largely sparing women, children, and non-German Europeans. Germany dispatched Lieutenant General Lothar von Trotha, who arrived in June and in October issued the infamous Vernichtungsbefehl (extermination order), commanding the destruction of the Herero nation. The subsequent campaign of military encirclement, deliberate denial of water, concentration camps, and forced labour killed an estimated 65,000–80,000 Herero (approximately 80% of the population). This is widely recognised by historians as the first genocide of the twentieth century.
key_figures: Samuel Maharero (Paramount Chief of the Herero)
Theodor Leutwein (Governor of German South-West Africa, replaced by von Trotha)

sources.name: Von Trotha's Vernichtungsbefehl (extermination order), 2 October 1904
German colonial administrative records, Reichskolonialamt archives
Jan-Bart Gewald, Herero Heroes: A Socio-Political History of the Herero of Namibia, 1890–1923 (1999)
David Olusoga and Casper W. Erichsen, The Kaiser's Holocaust: Germany's Forgotten Genocide (2010)

events.certainty_note: Well documented through German military and administrative records, survivor testimony, and extensive modern scholarship. Exact death tolls are estimates due to the nature of the violence.

events.title: Entente Cordiale signed between Britain and France
events.description: On 8 April 1904, Britain and France signed the Entente Cordiale, a series of agreements resolving longstanding colonial disputes — most critically, France acknowledged British predominance in Egypt while Britain recognised French interests in Morocco. The agreements ended centuries of Anglo-French colonial rivalry and marked a fundamental realignment of European diplomacy, driven in part by shared anxiety over German imperial ambitions under Kaiser Wilhelm II. Though not a formal military alliance, the Entente provided the diplomatic framework that would deepen into military coordination and ultimately draw Britain into the First World War alongside France in 1914. It also facilitated the later Triple Entente with Russia.
key_figures: Edward VII (King of the United Kingdom, whose 1903 Paris visit helped warm relations)

sources.name: Text of the Anglo-French Declarations, 8 April 1904 (published in treaty collections)
P.J.V. Rolo, Entente Cordiale: The Origins and Negotiation of the Anglo-French Agreements of 8 April 1904 (1969)
Christopher Andrew, Théophile Delcassé and the Making of the Entente Cordiale (1968)

events.certainty_note: Treaty texts survive in full; diplomatic correspondence extensively preserved in both British and French archives.

events.title: British Younghusband Expedition reaches Lhasa
events.description: A British military expedition under Colonel Francis Younghusband, authorised by Viceroy Lord Curzon, invaded Tibet and reached the capital Lhasa on 3 August 1904. The expedition was motivated by British fears of Russian influence in Tibet and the desire to secure the northern frontier of India. Tibetan resistance was crushed with devastating effect — at the Battle of Guru on 31 March, British Maxim guns killed an estimated 600–700 Tibetan soldiers in what was effectively a massacre. The 13th Dalai Lama fled to Mongolia. The resulting Anglo-Tibetan Convention (September 1904) opened Tibet to British trade and effectively denied it independent diplomatic relations, though the agreement was later modified under pressure from China and Russia.

sources.name: Francis Younghusband, India and Tibet (1910) — participant memoir
British India Office Records, correspondence of Lord Curzon
Charles Allen, Duel in the Snows: The True Story of the Younghusband Mission to Lhasa (2004)

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented through British military and administrative records. Tibetan perspectives are less well preserved in written form but have been recovered through later scholarship.

events.title: Siege of Port Arthur begins
events.description: Following the initial naval attack, Japanese forces under General Nogi Maresuke began the siege of the heavily fortified Russian naval base at Port Arthur in August 1904. The siege was one of the bloodiest engagements of the Russo-Japanese War, foreshadowing the trench warfare and attritional tactics of the First World War. Japanese forces launched repeated frontal assaults against entrenched positions defended by machine guns, barbed wire, and modern artillery, suffering catastrophic casualties — over 57,000 Japanese killed and wounded before the fortress's surrender in January 1905. The siege demonstrated that modern defensive technology had outpaced offensive tactics, a lesson largely ignored by European military planners.
key_figures: General Kondratenko (Russian chief of defence, killed December 1904)

sources.name: War correspondent reports (Ellis Ashmead-Bartlett, Richard Barry, and others), 1904–1905
Japanese General Staff, Official History of the Russo-Japanese War (multiple volumes)
Denis and Peggy Warner, The Tide at Sunrise: A History of the Russo-Japanese War (1974)

events.certainty_note: One of the most thoroughly reported military engagements of the pre-1914 era, covered by international press correspondents and military attachés from numerous countries.

events.title: United States takes control of Panama Canal construction
events.description: On 4 May 1904, the United States formally took control of the Panama Canal Zone and the assets of the failed French canal company. The transfer followed the controversial US-backed Panamanian secession from Colombia in November 1903 and the Hay–Bunau-Varilla Treaty. Chief Engineer John Findley Wallace arrived in June to begin organising the massive construction effort. The project, which would take a decade to complete, represented an unprecedented feat of engineering and logistics, requiring the excavation of approximately 170 million cubic metres of earth and the construction of a lock-based canal system. It was also marked by significant labour exploitation, particularly of Caribbean workers, and continued American imperial influence in Central America.
key_figures: Theodore Roosevelt (President of the United States)

sources.name: Hay–Bunau-Varilla Treaty (1903) and Isthmian Canal Commission records, 1904
David McCullough, The Path Between the Seas: The Creation of the Panama Canal, 1870–1914 (1977)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented through US government records, engineering reports, and press coverage.

events.title: Theodore Roosevelt wins presidential election in a landslide
events.description: On 8 November 1904, Theodore Roosevelt won the presidential election in his own right, defeating Democratic candidate Alton B. Parker by one of the largest margins in American history (336 to 140 electoral votes, 56.4% of the popular vote). Roosevelt had acceded to the presidency upon William McKinley's assassination in 1901 and used his first term to establish a reputation as a progressive reformer and trust-buster. His decisive victory gave him a strong mandate for his second term, during which he would pursue the Hepburn Act, the Pure Food and Drug Act, and an assertive foreign policy including the Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine. On election night, he pledged not to seek a third term — a promise he later regretted.
key_figures: Alton B. Parker (Democratic candidate, Chief Judge of New York)

sources.name: Official election returns, 1904; contemporary newspaper coverage (New York Times, Washington Post)
Edmund Morris, Theodore Rex (2001)

events.certainty_note: Fully documented through official election records and extensive press coverage.

events.title: New York City subway opens
events.description: On 27 October 1904, New York City's first underground rapid transit line, the Interborough Rapid Transit (IRT) subway, opened to the public. The initial line ran from City Hall to 145th Street in Harlem, covering 9.1 miles with 28 stations. Over 100,000 passengers rode on the first day. The subway transformed New York's geography, enabling the development of the outer boroughs and accelerating the city's growth into the world's largest metropolis. It was a triumph of progressive-era urban planning and engineering, built largely by immigrant labour under often dangerous conditions, and set the pattern for mass transit systems in cities worldwide.
key_figures: August Belmont Jr. (financier, president of IRT)
George B. McClellan Jr. (Mayor of New York)

sources.name: Contemporary press coverage, New York Times, 28 October 1904
Clifton Hood, 722 Miles: The Building of the Subways and How They Transformed New York (1993)

events.certainty_note: Precisely dated through official records and extensive press coverage.

events.title: FIFA (Fédération Internationale de Football Association) founded
events.description: On 21 May 1904, representatives from seven European football associations — France, Belgium, Denmark, the Netherlands, Spain, Sweden, and Switzerland — founded the Fédération Internationale de Football Association (FIFA) in Paris. The organisation was created to provide a single governing body for international football (soccer) competitions, as the English Football Association had shown little interest in organising international play. Robert Guérin of France became the first president. Though initially a modest European body, FIFA would grow into one of the most powerful international sports organisations, governing the world's most popular sport and organising the World Cup from 1930 onward.
key_figures: Robert Guérin (French journalist, first FIFA president)
Carl Anton Wilhelm Hirschman (Dutch representative, key organiser)

sources.name: FIFA founding documents and statutes, 21 May 1904
David Goldblatt, The Ball is Round: A Global History of Football (2006)

events.certainty_note: Founding date and participants precisely documented in FIFA's own archives.

events.title: Battle of Yalu River — first major land battle of the Russo-Japanese War
events.description: On 30 April – 1 May 1904, Japanese forces under General Kuroki Tamemoto defeated a Russian force under General Zasulich at the Yalu River, the border between Korea and Manchuria. It was the first major land engagement of the Russo-Japanese War and the first significant defeat of a European army by an Asian power in modern times. The Japanese demonstrated superior tactical coordination, effective use of artillery, and aggressive infantry tactics. The victory secured the Japanese flank and allowed the advance into Manchuria. International military observers from numerous countries witnessed the battle, and their reports influenced military thinking worldwide.
key_figures: General Kuroki Tamemoto (Japanese First Army commander)
General Mikhail Zasulich (Russian Eastern Detachment commander)

sources.name: Reports of foreign military attachés (British, German, American) present at the battle
The Official History of the Russo-Japanese War (British Committee of Imperial Defence, 1910)

events.certainty_note: Witnessed and reported by numerous international military observers and war correspondents.

events.title: Louisiana Purchase Exposition (St. Louis World's Fair) opens
events.description: The Louisiana Purchase Exposition opened on 30 April 1904 in St. Louis, Missouri, commemorating the centennial of the 1803 Louisiana Purchase (delayed one year). It was the largest world's fair to date, covering nearly 1,300 acres with over 1,500 buildings and attracting approximately 20 million visitors over seven months. The fair showcased industrial and technological achievements but was also deeply embedded in the racial ideology of its era, featuring 'anthropological' exhibits that displayed indigenous peoples from the Philippines, Africa, and the Americas in staged 'villages' intended to demonstrate theories of racial hierarchy. The 1904 Summer Olympics were held concurrently, though poorly organised and overshadowed by the fair.
key_figures: David R. Francis (president of the exposition)
W.J. McGee (head of the anthropology department, organised the human exhibits)

sources.name: Official catalogues and administrative records of the Louisiana Purchase Exposition, 1904
Robert W. Rydell, All the World's a Fair: Visions of Empire at American International Expositions, 1876–1916 (1984)
Contemporary press coverage (St. Louis Post-Dispatch, Harper's Weekly)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented through official records, photographs, and press coverage.

events.title: Dogger Bank Incident nearly triggers Anglo-Russian war
events.description: On the night of 21–22 October 1904, the Russian Second Pacific Squadron under Admiral Rozhestvensky, en route from the Baltic to the Far East, fired on British fishing trawlers near the Dogger Bank in the North Sea, mistaking them for Japanese torpedo boats. One trawler was sunk and several fishermen killed. The incident caused an international crisis, with Britain mobilising the Royal Navy and threatening war. Russia was forced to accept international arbitration at The Hague, which found Russia at fault and ordered compensation. The incident further damaged Russia's international reputation, illustrated the panic and incompetence plaguing the Russian fleet, and demonstrated how colonial rivalries could cascade into great-power confrontation.

sources.name: Hague International Commission of Inquiry report on the North Sea Incident, 1905
British Admiralty records and diplomatic correspondence, October–November 1904
Constantine Pleshakov, The Tsar's Last Armada (2002)

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented through diplomatic records, the Hague inquiry, and press coverage in both Britain and Russia.

events.title: John Ambrose Fleming invents the thermionic valve (vacuum tube diode)
events.description: In November 1904, John Ambrose Fleming, professor at University College London and consultant to the Marconi Company, patented the thermionic valve (vacuum tube diode), the first practical electronic device. Building on Thomas Edison's observation of current flow in evacuated bulbs (the Edison effect), Fleming developed a two-electrode vacuum tube that could detect radio signals far more reliably than existing coherer-based detectors. The invention marked the birth of electronics as a distinct field and was the foundational technology for radio communication, early computers, and all electronic amplification until the transistor era. Lee de Forest would add a third electrode (the triode) in 1906, enabling amplification.
key_figures: John Ambrose Fleming (physicist and electrical engineer)

sources.name: British Patent No. 24,850, 'Improvements in Instruments for Detecting and Measuring Alternating Electric Currents,' filed 16 November 1904
J.A. Fleming, 'On the Conversion of Electric Oscillations into Continuous Currents by Means of a Vacuum Valve,' Proceedings of the Royal Society, 1905

events.certainty_note: Patent records provide precise dating. The significance of the invention for electronics is established historiographic consensus.

events.title: Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine proclaimed
events.description: In his annual message to Congress on 6 December 1904, President Theodore Roosevelt articulated what became known as the Roosevelt Corollary to the Monroe Doctrine. He asserted that the United States had the right to intervene in Latin American nations guilty of 'chronic wrongdoing' or inability to maintain order, particularly to prevent European powers from using debt collection as a pretext for intervention (as had nearly occurred in Venezuela in 1902–03). The Corollary effectively transformed the Monroe Doctrine from a defensive shield against European colonialism into a justification for American interventionism across the Western Hemisphere. It underwrote decades of US military interventions in the Caribbean and Central America.
key_figures: Theodore Roosevelt (President of the United States)
Elihu Root (Secretary of War, later Secretary of State)

sources.name: Theodore Roosevelt, Annual Message to Congress, 6 December 1904 (published in Congressional Record)
Serge Ricard, 'The Roosevelt Corollary,' Presidential Studies Quarterly, vol. 36 (2006)

events.certainty_note: The text of Roosevelt's message is preserved verbatim in the Congressional Record.

events.title: Abbey Theatre opens in Dublin
events.description: On 27 December 1904, the Abbey Theatre opened in Dublin with performances of W.B. Yeats's On Baile's Strand and Lady Gregory's Spreading the News. The theatre grew out of the Irish Literary Theatre movement and was funded by English heiress Annie Horniman. Under the direction of Yeats and Lady Gregory, the Abbey became the first state-subsidised theatre in the English-speaking world and a central institution of the Irish cultural revival, staging works in both English and Irish that explored Irish identity, mythology, and social conditions. It would become internationally famous and play a significant role in the broader movement toward Irish independence.

sources.name: Opening night programmes and press reviews, December 1904
Robert Welch, The Abbey Theatre, 1899–1999: Form and Pressure (1999)

events.certainty_note: Precisely dated through theatre records and contemporary press coverage.

events.title: General Slocum disaster — deadliest New York City event before 9/11
events.region: United States (New York City, East River)
events.description: On 15 June 1904, the steamboat General Slocum caught fire and sank in the East River during a church excursion organised by St. Mark's Evangelical Lutheran Church. An estimated 1,021 of the approximately 1,342 passengers — mostly women and children from the German-American community of Kleindeutschland (Little Germany) on the Lower East Side — perished. The disaster was caused by a combination of criminal negligence: rotten life preservers, inaccessible lifeboats, and a captain who failed to beach the vessel promptly. The tragedy devastated the Kleindeutschland community, which largely dispersed in its aftermath, and spurred reforms in steamboat safety regulations.
key_figures: Captain William Van Schaick (convicted of criminal negligence)

sources.name: US Steamboat Inspection Service investigation records and federal trial transcripts, 1904–1906
Contemporary press coverage (New York Times, New York Herald), June 1904
Edward T. O'Donnell, Ship Ablaze: The Tragedy of the Steamship General Slocum (2003)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented through official inquiries, trial records, and press coverage. Exact death toll varies slightly by source.

events.title: France passes law separating church and state
events.description: Throughout 1904, the French government under Prime Minister Émile Combes pursued an aggressive programme of laïcité (secularism) that culminated in the breaking of diplomatic relations with the Vatican in July and set the stage for the landmark 1905 law on the separation of church and state. In March 1904, religious orders were banned from teaching, and approximately 2,500 Catholic schools were closed. The conflict was driven by the legacy of the Dreyfus Affair, which had aligned much of the Catholic hierarchy with anti-republican forces. These measures represented the most decisive implementation of secular governance in European history to that point and established the French model of laïcité that endures today.

sources.name: French parliamentary debates and legislation, Journal Officiel, 1904
Maurice Larkin, Church and State after the Dreyfus Affair: The Separation Issue in France (1974)

events.certainty_note: Legislative records and diplomatic correspondence fully preserved.

events.title: Khedive Abbas II of Egypt sidelined; Lord Cromer consolidates British control
events.description: In 1904, Lord Cromer's effective dictatorship over Egypt deepened as the Entente Cordiale formally secured French recognition of British predominance there. The agreement removed the primary diplomatic obstacle to full British control. Khedive Abbas II, who had attempted to assert Egyptian autonomy, was increasingly marginalised. Meanwhile, the Egyptian nationalist movement was growing under figures like Mustafa Kamil, whose newspaper al-Liwa criticised British occupation and called for Egyptian self-governance. The year marked a consolidation of the colonial arrangement that would persist until the 1919 revolution and formal independence in 1922.
key_figures: Lord Cromer (British Agent and Consul-General in Egypt)
Khedive Abbas II (ruler of Egypt, nominal)
Mustafa Kamil (Egyptian nationalist leader and journalist)

sources.name: Lord Cromer, Annual Report on Egypt, 1904 (published as parliamentary papers)
Afaf Lutfi al-Sayyid Marsot, Egypt and Cromer: A Study in Anglo-Egyptian Relations (1968)

events.certainty_note: Well documented through British administrative records and Egyptian press. Exact dynamics of Khedive's marginalisation are interpreted from multiple overlapping sources.

events.title: Russo-Japanese War triggers Korean neutrality crisis; Japan tightens control over Korea
events.description: Korea declared neutrality at the outbreak of the Russo-Japanese War in January 1904, but Japan ignored this declaration and landed troops at Incheon on 8 February, the same day as the Port Arthur attack. On 23 February, Japan forced the Korean government to sign a protocol effectively making Korea a Japanese protectorate in all but name, granting Japan the right to station troops and use Korean territory. In August, the first Japan-Korea Agreement further placed Korean foreign affairs under Japanese 'advice.' Emperor Gojong's attempts to appeal to international powers were rebuffed. These agreements laid the direct groundwork for Japan's formal protectorate (1905) and annexation (1910) of Korea.
key_figures: Yi Yong-ik (Korean court official, attempted resistance)

sources.name: Text of the Japan-Korea Protocol, 23 February 1904
Peter Duus, The Abacus and the Sword: The Japanese Penetration of Korea, 1895–1910 (1995)

events.certainty_note: Treaty texts and diplomatic records survive in both Japanese and Korean archives.

events.title: Completion of the Trans-Siberian Railway
events.description: In 1904, the Trans-Siberian Railway became operational along its full length from Moscow to Vladivostok via the Chinese Eastern Railway through Manchuria, though a continuous route entirely on Russian territory (the Circum-Baikal section around Lake Baikal) was completed in September. At approximately 9,300 kilometres, it was the longest railway in the world. The railway was strategically vital during the Russo-Japanese War, though its single-track nature severely limited the speed at which Russia could reinforce its Far Eastern forces. It transformed Siberian settlement patterns, opened Siberian resources to exploitation, and fundamentally altered the strategic geography of Northeast Asia.
key_figures: Sergei Witte (former Finance Minister, chief architect of the railway project)

sources.name: Russian Ministry of Transport administrative records, 1904
Steven G. Marks, Road to Power: The Trans-Siberian Railroad and the Colonization of Asian Russia, 1850–1917 (1991)

events.certainty_note: Construction milestones are precisely documented. The exact 'completion' date is debated because different segments became operational at different times; the Circum-Baikal link closing the last all-Russian gap opened in September 1904.

events.title: Nama uprising joins Herero resistance in German South-West Africa
events.description: In October 1904, following the German military's devastating campaign against the Herero in the north, the Nama people of southern German South-West Africa rose in revolt under leaders including Hendrik Witbooi and Jakob Morenga. Witnessing the genocidal treatment of the Herero and facing similar colonial pressures of land expropriation and forced labour, the Nama launched a guerrilla campaign that proved far more tactically effective than the Herero's conventional resistance. The Nama uprising would continue until 1908, with Morenga earning recognition as a skilled guerrilla commander. German repression ultimately killed an estimated half of the Nama population through combat, concentration camps, and forced labour.
key_figures: Hendrik Witbooi (Nama captain, leader of the Witbooi Nama)
Jakob Morenga (guerrilla leader, called 'the Black Napoleon')

sources.name: German colonial military reports, Schutztruppe records, 1904–1908
Tilman Dedering, 'The German-Herero War of 1904: Revisionism of Genocide or Genocidal War?' Journal of Southern African Studies, vol. 19, no. 1 (1993)

events.certainty_note: Documented through German military records. Nama oral traditions and later testimonies supplement the colonial record.

events.title: Helen Keller graduates from Radcliffe College
events.description: In June 1904, Helen Keller graduated cum laude from Radcliffe College, becoming the first deafblind person to earn a bachelor's degree. Keller, who had lost her sight and hearing at 19 months of age, had been educated through the groundbreaking work of her teacher Anne Sullivan. Her graduation was an international sensation and became a powerful symbol for disability rights and the potential of education. Keller went on to become a prominent author, lecturer, political activist, and advocate for socialism, women's suffrage, and workers' rights — dimensions of her life often overshadowed by the inspirational narrative of her education.

sources.name: Radcliffe College records, commencement 1904
Helen Keller, The Story of My Life (1903, with supplementary materials through 1904)
Kim E. Nielsen, The Radical Lives of Helen Keller (2004)

events.certainty_note: Precisely documented through college records and extensive contemporary press coverage.

disconfirming_evidence: Several commonly repeated claims about 1904 events warrant qualification. (1) The Russo-Japanese War is often framed as the 'first time' an Asian power defeated a European one in modern warfare, but this framing obscures the Ethiopian victory over Italy at Adwa in 1896 and earlier instances of non-European military success against European forces; it is more accurate to say it was the first such defeat of a major European power in a full-scale industrialised war. (2) The characterisation of the Port Arthur attack as a 'sneak attack' or 'Pearl Harbor precedent' is debated: Japan had broken diplomatic relations before the attack, and the sequence of events is contested — some historians argue Russia was effectively warned, while others maintain Japan deliberately struck before the formal declaration of war reached St Petersburg. (3) The Herero genocide death toll of ~80% is widely cited but rests on population estimates that are themselves uncertain; while the genocide is not disputed, precise figures are approximations. (4) The founding of FIFA is sometimes described as a response to British indifference, but recent scholarship by Goldblatt and others shows the English FA was involved in preliminary discussions and the narrative of pure British disinterest is somewhat overstated. (5) The Roosevelt Corollary, while often presented as a dramatic doctrinal innovation, built on interventionist precedents already established during the Venezuelan crisis of 1902–03 and earlier American actions in the Caribbean.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 1904 is exceptionally rich, with extensive archival documentation available in multiple languages including government records, diplomatic correspondence, press coverage, personal memoirs, and photographs. The major bias is Eurocentric and imperial: most surviving sources were produced by or for colonial governments and metropolitan audiences. The Herero and Nama perspective is reconstructed largely from German records and later oral testimony. Korean perspectives on the Japanese encroachment are better preserved than many colonised peoples' records but were suppressed during the colonial period. The Russo-Japanese War was the first major conflict extensively covered by international war correspondents and military attachés, providing unusually diverse contemporary accounts but still filtered through the observers' own national and racial assumptions. Coverage of Sub-Saharan Africa, Central Asia, and rural populations in Asia and Latin America remains thin relative to events in Europe, North America, and areas of great-power conflict.

graph_edges.note: The Russo-Japanese War begun in 1904 ended with the Treaty of Portsmouth in September 1905
Russian military failures in the Russo-Japanese War contributed to domestic unrest culminating in the 1905 Revolution
The Herero and Nama genocide is now recognised as a precursor event in the history of genocide and international humanitarian law
The Anglo-French Entente Cordiale was extended to include Russia in 1907, forming the Triple Entente
The Entente Cordiale was a key link in the alliance system that drew Britain into WWI alongside France
The Entente's recognition of French interests in Morocco provoked Germany's challenge in the 1905 Moroccan Crisis
US takeover of canal construction in 1904 led to completion and opening in August 1914
The fleet involved in the Dogger Bank incident continued to the Far East and was annihilated at Tsushima in May 1905
The Roosevelt Corollary was a direct response to the European naval intervention against Venezuela in 1902–03
The 1904 Japan-Korea protocols were the immediate precursors to the formal protectorate established in 1905
Japanese control established in 1904 led directly to full annexation in 1910
The Younghusband Expedition was partly motivated by the same Anglo-Russian imperial rivalry that underlay the Russo-Japanese War
Fleming's vacuum tube diode was the direct precursor to de Forest's triode, enabling electronic amplification
The 1904 anti-clerical measures under Combes set the stage for the definitive 1905 separation of church and state
